title: Risk to Canadians low from Ebola and hantavirus, top doctor says, but new screening in place at airports
full_text:
No Ebola cases in Canada and no new hantavirus cases, chief public health officer says
The Latest
- Canada's chief public health officer, Dr. Joss Reimer, delivered an update on Ebola and the Andes hantavirus Friday.
- Reimer said there are no Ebola cases in Canada, and there have been no new hantavirus cases in Canada since the case announced last week.
- The Public Health Agency of Canada (PHAC) has previously said it is not implementing a travel ban or actively testing travellers for Ebola, though Ontario's Health Ministry said a resident who recently travelled to East Africa was tested for Ebola "out of an abundance of caution."
- World Health Organization Director General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus said this morning the Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo is spreading, prompting the organization to change its risk assessment to very high for the country but still low at the global level.
- PHAC confirmed a positive case of hantavirus last week in a Canadian isolating in British Columbia after leaving the cruise ship affected by a deadly outbreak.
Updates
May 22
Maia TustonicThe latest on Ebola and hantavirus: what we learned
We're wrapping up our live coverage after Canada's top doctor provided the latest updates on the Ebola and hantavirus outbreaks. Here are the main things to know:
While the risk level for Ebola is very high in the Democratic Republic of Congo, it remains low at the global and Canadian levels, according to World Health Organization Director General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus.
There are no Ebola cases in Canada, according to Chief Public Health Officer Dr. Joss Reimer.
An Ontario resident who was being monitored is negative for Ebola after a test was conducted out of an "abundance of caution," Reimer said. Meanwhile, the Congolese national on the flight that was diverted to Montreal on Wednesday did not have symptoms and has returned to their "point of origin," she added.
There are currently no Canadian travel bans for countries hit by Ebola, Reimer said, but the Public Health Agency of Canada has been implementing "enhanced screening" measures at the border as of Wednesday for the disease.
Travellers coming into Canada will face additional questions about whether they are coming from affected countries and if they are showing symptoms, Reimer said. Only those who have symptoms will be tested for Ebola. More staff, including quarantine officers, will be at several points of entry, she added.
On the Andes hantavirus front, Reimer confirmed that there are no new cases in Canada. The only positive case remains that of a Canadian who was isolating in B.C. after leaving the cruise ship hit by the outbreak, and is now in hospital.
Reimer repeatedly emphasized that the threat of both Ebola and hantavirus in Canada is low, and that Canada is ready to respond to any detected risks if needed.
We’ll continue following this story at cbc.ca/news
- Jennifer La Grassa
Do enhanced screening measures and more signs at airports actually work?
Federal health officials repeatedly said they have added enhanced screening measures and signs at airports to help protect Canadians.
It might not seem like much, but screening is one of the best tools we have, says Dr. Gerald Evans, medical director of infection, prevention and control at Kingston Health Sciences Centre in Ontario.
When people are assessed for symptoms and then monitored, it ensures they are being tracked, said Evans.
Doing so "allows us better control" in responding to any possible cases, he said.
But it also requires strong communication between federal authorities and provincial or local health units.
He also said screening shouldn't just happen upon entry, but also when people are leaving other areas.
Signs at airports, on the other hand, usually aren't all that helpful, Evans said.
"You're deplaning … you're tired, you just came back from a long trip. The last thing you are doing is looking at that signage to decide if you're supposed to say something," he said.
- Jennifer La Grassa
What measures are in place ahead of events like the FIFA World Cup?
The first Canadian FIFA World Cup 2026 games will take place in Toronto on June 12 and Vancouver on June 13.
"Given the growing outbreak and the expected high volume of travellers associated with FIFA, ongoing vigilance is critical," PHAC said in an email.
It added that Canada has already implemented measures, including:
- Enhanced health screening for travellers entering Canada who have been in the affected areas, and signage at airports.
- Updated travel advisories for the impacted regions.
"The Government of Canada is closely monitoring the outbreak of Ebola virus disease in the Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda through collaboration with international partners, including the World Health Organization," reads part of PHAC's statement.
It continued to say that while the WHO has said the risk is high in the areas where the outbreak is currently taking place, the risk is "low at the global level."
PHAC said while it's already done an infectious disease assessment of imported risks associated with the FIFA World Cup, it is also preparing a rapid risk assessment, what it called a "quick, evidence-based investigation used by public health agencies to evaluate acute health threats."
- Jennifer La Grassa
What protocols does Canada have in place?
PHAC says it has increased health screening for travellers into Canada from the impacted regions and added signs at airports. It also updated its travel guidance yesterday, now recommending that Canadians avoid non-essential travel to D.R.C.
According to Dr. Vinh, travellers should also:
- Disclose at the border if they may have been exposed to the virus, even if they don't have symptoms.
- Isolate and contact public health if they develop any symptoms.
He also said that health-care workers should be mindful of the ongoing outbreak and pay attention to patients who come in with symptoms and may have recently travelled.
- Jennifer La Grassa
Is there a vaccine for this strain of Ebola?
There are currently no treatments or vaccines available for the Bundibugyo strain of the Ebola virus. This is what makes the current outbreak concerning to health officials, with the WHO calling it "extraordinary."
While there's no current vaccine candidate, there are ongoing clinical trials to develop and fast track approvals.
The Zaire strain of the virus, which is the one that most frequently circulates in D.R.C., does have a vaccine. It's called the Ervebo, but is only effective against the Zaire strain.
- Jennifer La Grassa
How does Ebola spread?
Ebola spreads through direct contact with bodily fluids, like blood, vomit or diarrhea, from someone who is sick or has died from the virus.
"Once you have symptoms, like vomiting, diarrhea, a fever … then you are contagious," said Dr. Donald Vinh, an infectious disease specialist at McGill University Health Centre in Montreal.
That means anyone who is asymptomatic — which means they are infected but don't have symptoms — is not "thought to be contagious," he added.
"Ebola is not spread through casual contact, and it's not airborne like measles or COVID," he said.
- Jennifer La Grassa
Do travel bans work to curb the spread of outbreaks?
In an email, Dr. Donald Vinh, an infectious disease specialist at McGill University Health Centre in Montreal, told me that the science doesn't "strongly favour broad travel/border bans."
"Studies … have repeatedly found that blanket travel restrictions often have limited impact on stopping the spread of Ebola internationally," he said.
He said it's because of a few reasons:
- The disease is not easily spread through casual contact.
- Infected travellers are identified through symptoms, contact tracing or monitoring.
"Researchers have also warned that strict travel bans can unintentionally disrupt humanitarian aid, reduce transparency from affected countries, and complicate outbreak response efforts," he said.
- Jennifer La Grassa
Why was the plane headed to the U.S. diverted to Canada?
Dr. Joss Reimer, Canada's chief public health officer, was asked Friday about potential travel bans and restrictions as cases of Ebola mount in Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda. That process, she said, rests with cabinet, though she noted she would provide advice.
Reimer said that's a question for Transport Canada.
"Those discussions do not fall within the Public Health Agency of Canada's purview," she said.
I have reached out to Transport Canada on this.
When asked whether Canada needs to put travel restrictions in place to protect against Ebola, Reimer did not directly answer. She said Canada has measures in place like enhanced screening and has prepared sites across the country to respond, if needed.
- Jennifer La Grassa
Should Canada have travel restrictions in place?
We're starting to answer some of the questions you have been sending in around Ebola. This is one of the most common ones we've received via email and social media.
When asked why Canada doesn't have travel restrictions in place, Reimer said that's a decision made by cabinet officials. She said implementing a ban would require them to assess whether that would be effective and whether there is evidence that the virus has spread outside of the region experiencing the outbreak.
Travel restrictions are a way to "slow the importation of cases," said Vinh.
But are they necessary?
"These measures can be temporarily useful to gain time, especially with the uncertainty of the outbreak that's going on regionally and the fragile health-care systems in the affected regions," he said.
"But historically, the most effective measures have been rapid detection, isolation of cases, contact tracing and protecting health-care workers."
He stressed that Canada has Ebola protocols in place and that "we're not anywhere in a situation that involves generalized border closures."
What questions do you have about Ebola? Send an email to ask@cbc.ca.
- Anya Zoledziowski
That's a wrap on the news conference
The news conference is officially over. But we've received a number of questions from our readers, so follow along for more information about the hantavirus and Ebola outbreaks.
